title: Coexisting Ballistic and Diffusive Heat Transport in Micrometer-Scale Molecular Junctions
authors: P. M. Martinez, O. Mateos-Lopez, J. C. Cuevas, J. G. Vilhena
abstract: Boltzmann transport theory, the standard framework for predicting thermal conductivity, assumes that every vibrational mode eventually scatters, acquiring a finite lifetime that yields a convergent, length-independent thermal conductivity: Fourier's law. Here we show that this assumption fails in a real molecular system. Through atomistic simulations of Au-alkane-Au single-molecule junctions spanning five orders of magnitude in length (0.5~nm to 4~$μ$m), we find that thermal conductivity never converges. Transport is ballistic for up to one hundred nanometers at room temperature, extending nearly two orders of magnitude beyond existing single-molecule measurements. Past this window, conductivity diverges as $L^{1/3}$, the scaling predicted by the Kardar-Parisi-Zhang universality class for momentum-conserving systems. Frequency-resolved decomposition of the heat current reveals the mechanism behind the divergence. Low-frequency acoustic modes never thermalize: protected by momentum conservation, they remain ballistic at every chain length, still carrying 50\% of the total heat current at $L = 2~μ$m. All other modes thermalize collectively as discrete vibrational states merge into scattering-active phonon bands with increasing length. Hence, the diverging conductivity emerges from the boundary between these coexisting transport regimes: as $L$ grows, the onset of scattering shifts progressively toward lower frequencies, suppressing the ballistic channel at a rate that sustains the $L^{1/3}$ divergence, leaving a finite contribution at every length. This coexistence of permanent ballistic and well-behaved diffusive transport, anticipated in abstract one-dimensional lattice models, survives the structural and chemical complexity of real micrometer-sized junctions.
summary: This paper demonstrates that heat transport in micrometer-scale molecular junctions does not follow Fourier's law, instead exhibiting superdiffusive scaling. The divergence in thermal conductivity is driven by low-frequency acoustic modes that remain ballistic even at large scales. This finding highlights a fundamental coexistence of ballistic and diffusive transport in 1D momentum-conserving systems.
main_problem: Investigating the breakdown of Fourier's law and the validity of the Boltzmann transport equation in quasi-1D molecular systems, specifically looking for the emergence of superdiffusive heat transport.
main_result: The study finds that thermal conductivity diverges as L^{1/3}, following the Kardar-Parisi-Zhang universality class, due to the coexistence of a persistent ballistic channel (low-frequency modes) and a diffusive sector.
method: Atomistic non-equilibrium molecular dynamics (NEMD) simulations combined with frequency-resolved spectral heat current decomposition and a 3-resistor model to isolate intrinsic conductance.
model_or_system: Au-alkane-Au single-molecule junctions spanning lengths from 0.5 nm to 4 micrometers, modeled using the OPLS-AA force field for the molecular chain and EAM for the gold electrodes.
key_observables: Thermal conductance (G_th), thermal conductivity (kappa_th), spectral heat current q(omega), vibrational density of states (VDoS), and spectral temperature T(R; omega).
important_parameters: Chain length (0.5 nm to 4 um), temperature (310 K), mechanical tension (0.35 nN to 1.05 nN), and frequency-dependent transport regimes (ballistic, superdiffusive, diffusive).
main_assumptions: Neglected Coulomb terms due to highly stretched conformations; reliance on classical molecular dynamics which may not capture all quantum effects at very low temperatures.
figures_summary: Schematic of the simulation setup; plots of thermal conductance and conductivity vs. length showing scaling regimes; spectral heat current and VDoS; and temperature profiles/spectral temperature analysis.
paper_structure: Introduction to the breakdown of Fourier's law; description of the NEMD simulation setup and molecular models; presentation of conductance scaling results; frequency-resolved analysis to identify the ballistic/diffusive coexistence; and discussion of universality and chemical insensitivity.
why_interesting: The paper explores non-equilibrium transport and the breakdown of standard kinetic theory in 1D systems, which is highly relevant to understanding anomalous diffusion and universality classes in many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ballistic-to-superdiffusive crossover and its spectral fingerprint in Au-alkane-Au junctions. a) Represen- tative Au-alkane-Au junction (310 K, 0.35 nN tension). Shading denotes harmonic restraints (gray) and thermal baths (green: 290 K, orange: 330 K). b) Total thermal conductance GMD th (navy, left axis) and intrinsic chain conductance Gchain th (cyan, right axis) as a function of chain length. Point dispersion is introduced for visualization purposes. c) Log-log plot of b). Dashed lines guide different scaling regimes: ballistic (L0), superdiffusive (L−2/3 or L−1/2), and diffusive (L−1). Error bars: standard deviation of 6 runs. d) Vibrational density of states (green) and...

FIG. 2. Enhancement of ballistic transport through mechanical pulling. a) Thermal conductance versus chain length under various applied tensions (averaged across 3 inde- pendent NEMD runs). Dashed lines indicate theoretical scal- ings for ballistic, superdiffusive, and diffusive regimes. Let- ters denote the specific systems detailed in (b) and (c). b) Cumulative thermal conductance for a representative 2 µm chain under low tension (⟨Fz⟩= 0.35 nN). The total con- ductance is decomposed in transverse (pink) and longitudinal (teal) components. c) Same as (b) for a highly stretched chain (⟨Fz⟩= 1.05 nN), showing the delayed onset of superdiffusion.

FIG. 3. Chain thermalization and persistent non- interacting modes in Au-alkane-Au junctions. a) Tem- perature profiles versus normalized coordinate (z/L) for vary- ing chain lengths. ∆T is the boundary temperature jump. b) Normalized spectral temperature ¯T(ω) for a 30 nm chain at 0.1L (blue), 0.5L (yellow), and 0.9L (red). Grey points de- note modes contributing < 0.5% to the total kinetic energy. Error bars: standard deviation over three 20 ns runs. c) Vi- brational density of states (green) and spectral heat current q(ω) (black) for the 30 nm chain, compared with the purely elastic limit (purple). d–g) As in (b,c) for 120 nm (d,e) and 500 nm (f,g) chains.
